Stakeholder Engagement in the Circular Economy: Building Trust and Reputation

[Blogs](https://circulareconomyalliance.com/category/cea-blogs/)

August 19, 2023

![Image 21: Stakeholder-Engagement-in-the-Circular-Economy_-Building-Trust-and-Reputation](https://circulareconomyalliance.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/Stakeholder-Engagement-in-the-Circular-Economy_-Building-Trust-and-Reputation.webp)

In the dynamic landscape of the circular economy, stakeholder engagement plays a pivotal role in driving sustainable change and building trust. As businesses transition from linear to circular models, effective engagement with stakeholders becomes essential for successful implementation. This potent article explores the significance of stakeholder engagement in the circular economy, emphasising its role in building trust and enhancing reputation. By forging meaningful relationships, listening to diverse perspectives, and fostering collaboration, organisations can navigate the complexities of the circular economy, inspire collective action, and foster a positive impact on the environment and society.

### The Importance of Stakeholder Engagement

Stakeholder engagement serves as a critical cornerstone of circular economy strategies, enabling organisations to understand and respond to the diverse needs and expectations of their stakeholders. Engaging with stakeholders fosters transparency, inclusivity, and shared responsibility, ensuring that the circular economy initiatives align with stakeholder interests and aspirations. By involving stakeholders throughout the decision-making process, organisations can harness their collective knowledge, gain valuable insights, and build a sense of ownership, resulting in increased acceptance and support for circular initiatives.

### Building Trust through Collaboration

In the circular economy, trust is a valuable currency that organisations must earn through active collaboration with stakeholders. By actively involving stakeholders in the co-creation of circular strategies, businesses demonstrate their commitment to shared goals and collective well-being. Collaborative partnerships with customers, suppliers, regulators, NGOs, and local communities enable organisations to foster trust, mitigate risks, and leverage diverse expertise. This collaboration not only enhances the quality and effectiveness of circular initiatives but also generates a sense of shared responsibility and accountability, contributing to the establishment of a trusted reputation.

### Listening to Diverse Perspectives

Effective stakeholder engagement in the circular economy requires organisations to actively listen and understand the diverse perspectives and concerns of stakeholders. By creating platforms for dialogue, organisations can capture a wide range of insights and incorporate them into their decision-making processes. Understanding stakeholder expectations, concerns, and aspirations allows organisations to tailor their circular strategies to address key issues, ensure inclusivity, and foster buy-in from stakeholders. Listening to diverse perspectives also enhances the organisation’s reputation as an empathetic and responsive entity, committed to addressing social and environmental challenges.

### Communication and Transparency

Open and transparent communication is crucial in building trust and reputation in the circular economy. Organisations need to effectively communicate their circular initiatives, progress, and impact to stakeholders, demonstrating transparency and accountability. Clear and concise messaging about the benefits, challenges, and outcomes of circular initiatives helps stakeholders understand the organisation’s commitment to sustainability. Transparent reporting on key metrics, environmental performance, and social impact reinforces the organisation’s credibility and builds trust with stakeholders.

### Measuring Impact and Celebrating Success

To maintain stakeholder trust and enhance reputation, organisations in the circular economy must measure and communicate their impact. By setting clear goals, establishing relevant metrics, and regularly monitoring and reporting progress, organisations demonstrate their commitment to accountable and sustainable practices. Celebrating successes, sharing lessons learned, and acknowledging challenges encountered further strengthen stakeholder engagement and trust. Transparently showcasing the positive social, environmental, and economic outcomes of circular initiatives generates confidence in the organisation’s ability to drive meaningful change.

### Conclusion

Effective stakeholder engagement is the bedrock of success in the circular economy. By actively involving stakeholders, building trust through collaboration, listening to diverse perspectives, and communicating transparently, organisations can navigate the complexities of the circular economy and build a positive reputation. As organisations embrace the circular economy, stakeholder engagement becomes a potent tool for driving sustainable change, fostering collective action, and leaving a lasting positive impact on the environment and society. By building trust and reputation through meaningful stakeholder engagement, organisations can forge a path towards a more circular and sustainable future.

Submitted By: Circular Economy Alliance Team

#### [We Welcome Franco Petocchi to Strategic Foresight Board!](https://circulareconomyalliance.com/cea-articles/franco-petocchi-sfb-member/)

[Articles](https://circulareconomyalliance.com/category/cea-articles/)

Franco brings a wealth of expertise, spanning over 25 years in consulting and industry leadership across the supply chain and logistics sectors, as well as a deep commitment to advancing circular economy principles globally. As the Managing Partner at Accirculate, a Brussels-based consulting firm specializing in Circular Economy and Circular Supply Chains, Franco has been at the forefront of driving transformative solutions.

Research article

Designing a stakeholder engagement framework with critical success factors for Hubs for Circularity
=====================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Aidana Tleuken a, Patricia Rogetzer a, Luca Fraccascia a b, Devrim Murat Yazan a

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jenvman.2025.125324](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jenvman.2025.125324 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0301479725013003&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •Analyzes drivers, barriers, and enablers affecting Hubs for Circularity (H4C) initiatives. 
*   •Identifies key stakeholder types in H4C context: Industry, Academia, Society, Policy. 
*   •Aligns H4C success factors with Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). 
*   •Introduces the FRONTIER framework for stakeholder engagement in H4C. 

Abstract
--------

This paper introduces a framework aimed at evaluating stakeholder involvement within Hubs for Circularity (H4C), which play a critical role in advancing the circular economy. It demonstrates the significance of collaboration among stakeholders and the need for a structured approach to assess the effectiveness of H4C initiatives. As a result of the literature review synthesis, the paper proposes a novel and actionable framework for stakeholder engagement. It is composed of several key elements: it begins with the identification and analysis of the different stakeholder groups participating in H4C projects. Next, it advocates for a comprehensive review of the factors influencing H4C implementation through an analysis of drivers, barriers, and enablers. It also emphasizes the development of success criteria aligned with the Sustainable Development Goals to measure the outcomes of H4C initiatives. The paper concludes by proposing future research directions, such as integrating the Societal Readiness Level and utilizing social network analysis and regional input–output modeling to assess the socio-economic impacts of H4C projects. Overall, the paper highlights the critical role of stakeholder engagement and effective evaluation frameworks in the success of H4C initiatives and the advancement of sustainability goals.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725015634)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725016147)

Keywords
--------

Hubs for Circularity

Industrial symbiosis

Circular economy

Socio-economic sustainability indicators

Stakeholder engagement

1. Introduction
---------------

As Earth’s resources face the threat of depletion, the need to adopt circular economy (CE) practices is rising, along with striving to follow and meet sustainable development goals (SDGs) in the industrial context. Adoption of CE in industries can help to create a sustainable approach to a product’s or resource’s life cycle by narrowing, slowing, and, eventually, closing the loop of resources. CE prolongs products’ lifespans, minimizing waste and maximizing resource efficiency through strategies such as reducing, reusing, and recycling [Bocken et al. (2016)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b16). The engagement of diverse stakeholders is critical in the development of CE initiatives, as it ensures that diverse needs and motivations are addressed. Various initiatives are expanding to address CE development in industries aiming to develop closed-loop practices. In general, these strategies can be categorized into hierarchical and grassroots strategies. Hierarchical (top-down) initiatives are usually structured and led by senior leadership designed from the ground up with a predefined purpose that encourages companies to collaborate towards a specific goal. For example, eco-industrial parks, promote industries’ collaboration among industries to adopt circular practices in the areas of industrial parks [Genc et al. (2019)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b54). Bottom-up approaches emerge through grassroots-level initiatives, leading to spontaneous development of industrial symbiosis networks such as the Kalundborg industrial symbiosis in Denmark, where regional businesses cooperate to exchange resources [Jacobsen, 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b70), [Fraccascia et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b51), [Saghafi and Roshandel, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b114).

### 1.1. Hubs for Circularity

The concept of Hubs for Circularity (H4C), introduced by SPIRE (Process4Planet) in 2021, demonstrates a blend of these top-down and bottom-up approaches [A.SPIREaisbl (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b5). The H4C concept presents an innovative approach within the sustainable development landscape that connects various local and regional stakeholders to jointly find regional synergies in the urban and rural ecosystem, develop solutions and accelerate the transition towards CE [Tsui et al. (2024)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b133), [Mendez Alva et al. (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b89). H4C, being a network of activities in regional central locations, have a goal to neutralize environmental degradation via the exchange of resources, services, infrastructure, and relevant information [A.SPIREaisbl (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b5), [Mendez Alva et al. (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b89). To achieve a self-supporting ecosystem, a broad range of regional stakeholders – from businesses to community groups, governments, and research institutions – must collaborate to implement regional-scale industrial partnerships and CE practices.

Different CE industrial initiatives exist, such as industrial symbiosis (IS), industrial parks, zero-waste hubs, urban–industrial symbiosis, sustainable production networks, positive energy districts, islands of sustainability, industrial recycling networks, and by-product synergies. Examples of different CE industrial initiatives include Landskrona in Sweden [Mirata and Emtairah (2005)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b92), Ulsan Mipo and Onsan in South Korea [Park et al. (2008)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b103), [Kumar et al. (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b76), Kymenlaakso in Finland [Sokka et al. (2011)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b120), Tianjin in Singapore [Caprotti (2014)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b26), Burnside in Canada [Wright et al. (2009)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b142), Gujiao in China [Song et al. (2018)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b121). All these concepts aim to foster circular and sustainable practices, reduce the negative ecosystem impacts and optimize resource use by networking with different stakeholders (industries and/or communities). However, they differ in their scales and level of stakeholder engagement. H4C largely involve the public community whereas other concepts such as industrial symbiosis, urban industrial symbiosis, eco-industrial parks, and positive energy districts focus more on industry collaboration. Moreover, H4C can be applied to broader contexts of locations (e.g., urban, industrial, rural) compared to concepts that are typically confined to industrial or urban regions. H4C is not limited to self-contained areas, in contrast to islands of sustainability. In addition, H4C has a broad scope of CE application in different contexts, without being limited to only waste (like zero-waste hubs) or energy (like positive energy districts). In summary, H4C stands out from other concepts due to their inclusive approach, which integrates comprehensive CE practices across various types of stakeholders, and develops solutions adaptable to different geographical and contextual settings. The definitions of these concepts can be seen in [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#tbl1), while [Table 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#tbl2) shows how H4C stands out among all these other concepts.

Table 1. CE industrial initiatives: key definitions.

| Concept name | Description | References |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Hubs for Circularity | Centers or networks (integrated ecosystems) that promote CE principles, where resources are utilized efficiently and waste is reduced through recycling, reusing, and sharing materials. | [A.SPIREaisbl (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b5), [Mendez Alva et al. (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b89) |
| Industrial Symbiosis | A collaborative and mutually beneficial relationship among industries where waste, by-products, resources, and energy are exchanged and utilized to improve overall efficiency and sustainability. | [Chertow (2000)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b32); [Iacondini et al. (2015)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b68), [Li et al. (2015)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b78), [Bacudio et al. (2016)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b9), [Yazan and Fraccascia (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b144) |
| Eco-Industrial park | An industrial park designed to minimize environmental impact and promote sustainability by integrating diverse businesses and industries to create synergies in resource use, waste management, and energy generation. | [Hong and Gasparatos (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b67), [Afshari et al. (2018)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b1), [Pilouk and Koottatep (2017)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b105), [Cawley (2017)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b28), [Farel et al. (2016)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b43) |
| Urban Symbiosis | A concept similar to industrial symbiosis but applied to urban environments, focusing on responsible resource management, minimizing waste, and cooperation between different stakeholders in cities. | [Butturi and Gamberini (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b23) |
| Sustainable Production Network | A system of interconnected businesses and organizations involved in the creation and delivery of products or services, often collaborating to optimize efficiency, reduce costs, and improve sustainability. | [Bernard et al. (2022)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b12), [Carvalho and Tahbaz-Salehi (2019)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b27) |
| By-Product Synergy | The practice of identifying and utilizing waste from one company as resources or inputs for another industry, thus, reducing waste and promoting circularity. | [Mangan (1997)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b83) |
| Islands of Sustainability | Specific areas or communities that demonstrate high levels of sustainability and can be used as models for larger-scale initiative development. | [Wallner et al. (1996)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b138) |
| Positive Energy Districts | Areas that produce more renewable energy than they consume, utilizing various technologies and strategies to diminish energy consumption and promote the use of sustainable energy sources. | [Clemente et al. (2019)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b36), [Hearn and Castaño-Rosa (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b62), [Cáceres et al. (2022)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b24), [Sareen et al. (2022)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b116), [Volpatti et al. (2024)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b136), [Derkenbaeva et al. (2022)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b39) |
| Zero-Waste hubs | Centers or communities that aim to eliminate waste by minimizing consumption, promoting recycling and reuse,and implementing resource-efficient practices to achieve zero waste sent to landfills or incineration. | [Kerdlap et al. (2019)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b71), [Zaman (2022)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b146) |
| Industrial Recycling Network | An ecosystem of interconnected industries and facilities that collaborate to recycle and reuse materials, waste streams, and by-products to reduce environmental impact in the industrial sector. | [Korhonen et al. (2002)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b75), [Schwarz and Steininger (1997)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b118), [Posch (2010)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b107) |

Table 2. Comparison of CE concepts.

| Concept | Approach | Scope | Stakeholders | Regional context |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Hub 4 Circularity | Bottom-up and top-down | Various | All | Regional |
| Industrial Symbiosis | Bottom-up | Various | Industry | Regional |
| Eco-Industrial Park | Top-down | Various | Industry, policy | Industrial |
| Urban Industrial Symbiosis | Bottom-up | Various | Industry | Urban |
| Sustainable Production network | Top-down | Various, related to product development | Industry, Policy | Regional |
| By-Product Synergy | Bottom-up | Waste | Industry | Regional |
| Island of Sustainability | Bottom-up | Various | Communities | Area/Community |
| Positive Energy Districts | Top-down and bottom-up | Energy | Energy industry, policy, community | District |
| Zero-Waste hubs | Bottom-up | Waste | Communities | Urban/Regional |
| Industrial Recycling Network | Top-down | Waste | Industry, policy | Industrial |

### 1.2. Stakeholder engagement

Developing CE in industries through various initiatives is crucial, as it can drive the industry towards greater sustainability. Effective assessment of H4C success requires stakeholder engagement and a theoretical framework for measuring this success. Getting stakeholders involved is key to creating a strong sustainability culture in companies shifting to CE [Salvioni and Almici (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b115). A stakeholder can be defined as an identifiable group or individual who might influence the realization of an organization’s aims or can be influenced by the organization [Li et al. (2017)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b79). Effective integration of CE principles in industries demands synchronized teamwork between a diverse range of stakeholders, encompassing policymakers, industry, and community members [Tutunchian (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b134). This collaboration, however, must also account for the barriers and motivations that influence stakeholders’ willingness to participate in CE initiatives like H4C. Thus, engaging stakeholders through participation, dialogue, and meeting expectations is pivotal for the success of circular business models and the shift towards CE. As a result, the role of the stakeholders in the development of CE is crucial, as discussed in the works of various scholars [Pinheiro et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b106), [van Langen et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b77), [Sarkis et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b117).

### 1.3. Gap of the literature and research questions

Extensive research explores theories and applications of different IS relationships [Mendez Alva et al. (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b89), [A.SPIREaisbl (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b5), [Chertow (2000)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b32); [Iacondini et al. (2015)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b68), [Li et al. (2015)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b78),[Bacudio et al. (2016)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b9), [Yazan and Fraccascia (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b144), [Fraccascia et al. (2019)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b50), and the benefits and risks of sustainable industrial development for stakeholders [Cohen-Rosenthal and Musnikow (2003)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b37), [Camilleri (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b25), [Mortensen et al. (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b95), [Diaz et al. (2024)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b41), [Tutunchian (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b134), [Henriques et al. (2022b)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b64), [Cecchin et al. (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b29), however, these studies predominantly focus on industrial players while overlooking the critical role of societal engagement in achieving circularity.

The innovative concept of H4C broadens the scope of industrial symbiosis by incorporating a full network of interested parties into the CE ecosystem. However, the literature lacks a structured framework for analyzing stakeholder collaboration comprehensively.

This study addresses this gap by developing a novel approach to stakeholder engagement in H4C. Previous research has focused exclusively on industrial players [Chrysikopoulos et al. (2024)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b33), assessing the progress of industrial symbiosis [Faria et al. (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b46), and examining social involvement in isolation [Faria et al. (2023a)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b44). In this framework, we adopt a holistic approach by emphasizing multi-actor collaboration, including community and policy participation. We comprehensively address the success factors for stakeholder engagement in H4C and align them to the UN SDGs. This research develops a structured understanding of H4C stakeholder networks, thus contributing to the design of inclusive and effective CE initiatives.

The main aim of this paper is to develop a framework to identify factors that lead to successful stakeholder engagement in H4C, with a focus on a meso- and macro-level perspective. This aim can be separated into the following research questions:

RQ1: What are the stakeholder types involved in H4C?

RQ2: What are the drivers, barriers, and enablers influencing the stakeholder engagement implementation of H4C, as identified through a structured literature analysis? Within this analysis, “drivers” push the advancement of circular practices, “barriers” present impediments to their implementation, and “enablers” facilitate their realization [Testa et al. (2017)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b130).

RQ3: Based on identified stakeholders, drivers, barriers, and enablers, what socio-economic success factors, aligned with SDGs, can be developed to measure the engagement of diverse stakeholders in H4C?

RQ4: How to effectively structure analysis of stakeholder engagement in H4C?

This analysis serves as a structured framework to explore the forces that influence the adoption and progress of circularity initiatives within H4C. After creating categories for drivers, barriers, and enablers, we identify socio-economic indicators and develop a list of success factors aligned with the DBE results. These factors will then be categorized aligning with SDGs.

The paper is structured as follows: Section [2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#sec2) presents the adopted methodology, and Section [3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#sec3) provides the results, which consist of both descriptive and content analysis. Section [4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#sec4) discusses the obtained results, offering insights into success factors for stakeholder engagement and suggesting potential avenues for further research.

2. Methodology
--------------

In this paper, the Systematic Literature Review (SLR) methodology was selected for this research study because it offers a methodical and comprehensive examination of existing literature, allowing for a holistic overview of related concepts [Rocco et al. (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b112). Before proceeding with the SLR, we reviewed various concepts similar to H4C to facilitate keyword selection (see [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#tbl1), [Table 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#tbl2)). Then, we followed four stages in the process of SLR: (1) Data collection, (2) Data filtering, (3) Descriptive analysis, followed by (4) Content analysis. The Scopus database was used as it covers a larger number of journals than other databases for the articles published after 1996 [Chadegani et al. (2013)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b31),[Falagas et al. (2008)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725013003#b42), which fits our scope.

During the data collection the following search in Scopus was used: “TITLE-ABS-KEY(“stakeholder”) AND TITLE-ABS-KEY(“circular economy” OR “sustainability” OR “sustainable”) AND TITLE-ABS-KEY(“industrial symbiosis” OR “hubs for circularity” OR “eco industrial park*” OR “eco-industrial park” OR “urban symbiosis” OR “production network” OR “industrial cluster” OR “circular* hub*” OR “circular* ecosystem” OR “by-product-synergy” OR “island* of sustainability” OR “ positive energy district*” OR “zero waste hub*” OR “industrial recycling network*”)”, which has resu

enriched_points.expanded_content: Involve diverse stakeholders through transparent dialogue and collaboration to enhance ownership, align interests, and foster trust in circular economy initiatives.
Organizations must integrate circular principles into their core operations, ensuring resource efficiency, waste reduction, and sustainable practices to achieve long-term environmental goals.
Allocate resources towards innovative technologies, sustainable projects, and partnerships that drive circular economy initiatives and contribute to resilience against environmental challenges.
